title: How to Rank on Google in Overseas Markets: SEO Guide for Indonesian Businesses
metaDescription: A complete SEO guide for Indonesian businesses wanting to rank on Google in overseas markets like Malaysia, Australia, Japan, UK, and the Middle East. Step-by-step strategies.
content:
How to Rank on Google in Overseas Markets: SEO Guide for Indonesian Businesses

SEO for Indonesian businesses overseas is fundamentally different from domestic SEO in Indonesia. When you want to rank on Google.com.au, Google.com.my, or Google.co.jp, the rules change — you are competing in local markets with different search behaviours, different competitors, and different ranking signals. This guide breaks down exactly what Indonesian businesses need to do to rank on Google in international markets.

Why Overseas SEO Is Different from Domestic SEO

In Indonesia, you are competing with other Indonesian businesses for Indonesian searchers on Google.co.id. When you go overseas, three things change simultaneously:

  • The Google domain changes — Google.com.au, Google.com.my, and Google.co.jp each prioritise local businesses and local content
  • The search language may change — your target customers may be searching in English, Malay, Arabic, or Japanese rather than Bahasa Indonesia
  • Local authority matters more — Google heavily weights local signals (local backlinks, local reviews, local business listings) when ranking results in a specific country

This means a website that ranks brilliantly in Indonesia will often rank nowhere in Australia or Saudi Arabia, unless specific international SEO steps are taken.

Step 1: Choose Your Geographic Target Carefully

Before you do any SEO work, decide which country you are optimising for. International SEO resources are finite, and trying to rank in five countries simultaneously without a clear priority order is a common and costly mistake. Start with one country, achieve rankings there, then expand.

To choose your primary target market, ask:

  • Where is the largest concentration of my potential customers?
  • What is the search volume for my key terms in each country?
  • How competitive is the market in each country?
  • Do I have any existing customers or connections in a specific country?

Step 2: Set Up Your Website for International Targeting

Google needs to understand which country your website is intended for. There are several technical signals you can use:

  • Country-specific domain — A .com.au domain signals to Google that your site is for Australia. A .com.my signals Malaysia. This is the strongest geotargeting signal available.
  • Subdirectory targeting — If you want to target multiple countries from one domain, use subdirectories: yourdomain.com/au/ for Australia, yourdomain.com/my/ for Malaysia. Use hreflang tags to tell Google which version is for which country and language.
  • Google Search Console geotargeting — If you use a generic .com domain, you can set a geotarget preference in Google Search Console. This is a softer signal but still useful.
  • Hosting location — While less important than it once was, hosting your site on servers in or near your target country can improve both rankings and page load speed.

Step 3: Keyword Research for Overseas Markets

The keywords that diaspora Indonesians search for overseas are often different from what Indonesians in Indonesia search for. You need to research keywords specifically in your target country and language combination.

For example, if you run an Indonesian restaurant:

  • In Malaysia: searches may be in Malay — "restoran indonesia kuala lumpur", "masakan nusantara KL"
  • In Australia: searches are typically in English — "Indonesian restaurant Sydney", "halal Indonesian food Melbourne"
  • In Saudi Arabia: searches may be in Arabic or English — "مطعم اندونيسي الرياض" or "Indonesian food Riyadh"

Use Google Keyword Planner with the target country selected, or tools like Ahrefs or Semrush with country filtering, to find the actual terms people use. Do not assume Indonesian search terms translate directly.

Step 4: Build Local Authority with Country-Specific Backlinks

Backlinks from websites in your target country are significantly more powerful for overseas rankings than links from Indonesian websites. To build local authority:

  • Get listed in local directories — Yellow Pages Australia, True Local, Yelp (for Western markets), Malaysian business directories for Malaysia, and equivalent directories in your target country
  • Earn mentions in local media — Reach out to local newspapers, food bloggers, and community websites in your target country
  • Partner with complementary local businesses — A halal food business in London might exchange links with a halal grocery store or a Muslim travel agency
  • Sponsor or participate in local Indonesian community events — These often result in coverage and links from community websites and social media

Step 5: Optimise Google Business Profile for Each Location

If you have a physical presence overseas — a restaurant, a shop, an office — Google Business Profile (formerly Google My Business) is essential. A fully optimised GBP listing can get you ranking in Google Maps and local search results without even needing to rank with your website.

For each overseas location:

  • Create a Google Business Profile in the target country
  • Fill in every field completely — name, address, phone, website, hours, photos, categories
  • Write a business description that includes your key target keywords
  • Actively encourage customers to leave Google reviews — the number and quality of reviews is a major local ranking factor
  • Post regular updates (photos, offers, events) to keep the profile active

Step 6: Create Localised Content for Your Target Market

Content that ranks in overseas markets needs to be written for that market, not simply translated from Indonesian. This means:

  • Using local currency, local units of measurement, and local cultural references
  • Addressing local concerns — Australians searching for Indonesian food care about halal certification differently than Saudis do
  • Writing in the local language — English for English-speaking markets, Malay for Malaysia, etc.
  • Covering topics that are relevant to your local audience, not just your home market

How Long Does Overseas SEO Take?

International SEO takes time. Expect the following timeline:

  • Months 1-2: Technical setup, keyword research, on-page optimisation, Google Business Profile
  • Months 3-4: Content creation, initial backlink building, first indexing signals
  • Months 5-6: First rankings for lower-competition keywords appear
  • Months 6-12: Rankings solidify, organic traffic begins to grow meaningfully
  • Month 12+: Compound growth as domain authority in the target country increases

The businesses that fail at overseas SEO almost always do so because they stop after 2-3 months without seeing results. SEO is a long-term investment, but the returns compound significantly over time.

Common Mistakes to Avoid

  • Using the same keywords as in Indonesia without local research
  • Building only Indonesian backlinks for an overseas-targeted site
  • Writing all content in Bahasa Indonesia for a market that searches in English
  • Ignoring Google Business Profile for businesses with a physical location
  • Expecting results in less than 6 months
  • Trying to target too many countries simultaneously with insufficient resources

Work With International SEO Experts

Overseas SEO done well requires experience with international search markets, multilingual keyword research, and technical implementation of hreflang and geotargeting.
